sentences.text: Geinia Weinstein-- G-E-I-N-E-I-A.
I probably should have said on-- they thought that.
[LAUGHS]
No.
Geineia.
Nornberg.
N-O-R-N-B-E-R-G.
No.
[NON-ENGLISH].
This is a Yiddish name.
Now 81-- my, God!
This is Brzesko Nove.
You want that I spell?
B-R-Z-E-S-K-O, N-O-V-E.
Poland.
This was, before the war, a very small town.
And over there, I went in the school not too long.
Then we moved to Przoszece.
P-R-Z-O-S-Z-E-C-E, Przoszece.
How?
Over there I went into school.
Once a week I worked in store.
You know, because it wasn't so good.
I have to make a living.
I go in the school and once a week in the store.
Oh.
I sell it-- sales.
My father was a special from the men-- the shoes-- not the boots.
Yeah.
He was a special.
Not orthodox, but we keep kosher, strictly kosher.
My mother was very kosher.
And everyone go in the shul and davened every day.
Oh, yeah.
It's not strictly like now the Orthodox.
And Shabbes is Shabbes.
You make Shabbes and light the candles, you know, and everything.
And they go in the shul, the men.
It was like this in our small town.
Well, it's like this.
We work.

I worked in a house then in-- in-- then in Krakow, not in-- and it was before Shabbes, everything is cleaned; make challahs, wine, fish has to be every week-- fish-- no, and chicken soup, and the like, you know.
Everything is like this in our small town.
And when they came from the shul, then we eat.
My father, my brother.
And I wasn't married this time.
I was young.
And this was in next day Shabbes, they went to the shul.
We didn't work.
No.
And we didn't cook in Shabbes-- no.
Oh, it's like this.
Then after the meal, we go to this certain town.
And that's the whole day it was.
At home I speak Yiddish and Polish-- Yiddish mostly, and Polish.
Oh, yeah.
Polish, too.
You know, a lot of people, they couldn't talk really Pole.
They just-- it's very difficult.
Yeah.
Polish language is very difficult.
Yeah.
Then in the school I have friends, you know-- Jewish and Polish.
I was-- I was very close to everybody.
Oh, yeah.
When I was young, I don't remember this.
And then when the war came, it was terrible.
They-- when--
When they came-- not from-- from-- Przoszece.
We moved to Krakow.
In Krakow--
In Krakow, I used to work by myself.
And I got one girl, one machine, there was two machines.
We work very hard.
No.
No.
My parents.
And the girl was-- lived by us, too, you know.
No.
I know her from the small town.
I bring her to Krakow, and she works with me the whole time.
Before the war, about two, three years before the war.
I think 1936, '35-- something like this.
And then-- when the war was?
When the war came--
We hear.
Oh, yeah.
When they was in Poland, we know, because we know everything.
The-- the-- the bombs-- everything.
And they came, right away.
You couldn't find anything.
No.
No, no-- like this, just like this.
And we-- no bread-- nothing.
There was a big, big line.
The Poland stays, too.
They take you from the line and throw you out.
And you have to go again and try, you know.
It was very, very hard to find some things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I got a sister in-- in small town, Brzeko.
Nowe.
She lives over there.
She got a store.
Tobel Raizel.
But I don't remember the name for her-- from her husband.
I don't.
And we went over there to her.
When we went-- yeah-- when the war-- then was in 1939.
And I says to my first husband, you go.
Leon.
Leon Gams.
And I says to him, you know what?
I can't go.
I'm not gonna leave my parents.
I staying with my parents and you go.
Where he want.
Everybody left on Lvov.
Lvov.
This was in the Russia-- us belong with-- for-- to Poland-- you know, Lvov.
But they went over there.
He was over there.
And then my brother, my youngest brother, was in--
--the army.
Moishe.
So he was over there, and they saw each other.
Then they lived together by Ukraine.
And she-- they used to work.
This was a long, long time when they wanna come back to Poland.
And I said, for what?
They wanna send him to Russia to bury and to this.
He says, no.
I want to go.
He want to go home.
He left me and my parent.
It was one day.
The Ukrainian got one day they can do with the Jewish people what they want.
She bringed the-- the-- [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- the [NON-ENGLISH] to the room.
She said she has Jewish people.
And then the--
She was a Ukrainian.
A woman.
She was a superintendent.
This is like she take care of the house, you know, everything.
So she bringed everybody over there, the Ukrainian.
They got the one day they can do with the Jewish people.
What they did-- my oldest brother was over there, too, but not in the same place.
Then he talks from them to-- he take his head on the wall.
He couldn't hear.
What they did, they cut pieces from them.
My oldest brother--
--was in Israel, but he passed away.
Yeah.
That's how I know.
And they got killed by them.
Yeah but then we left to the small town to my sister over there.
We-- we was thinking maybe they not coming over there.
But they was over there-- all over.
It was very hard-- not so easy.
I don't know how many-- two months.
And what I got everything-- and my brother, I give everything to one guy.
He was the chief from the police.
And he tell us, this night, you have to go from the town.
Because they are going here.
And we left, and the small kids, my sister's three small kids, my mother, my father, and I.
We didn't-- where are you gonna go?
No place.
Who will let you in?
Nobody.
But one guy let us in where they caught the-- the horses, the cow-- one night.
And then in the night we hear what was going on-- the small town.
They was killed.
This family was killed-- everybody.
No.
No.
No.
This was in our village-- in our village.
Next day, he says, you have to go.
Where we go?
No place to go.
We go again to one.
I told you, my father got the money.
He borrowed then the money.
And he let him in-- but not for long-- just the same place where the cow-- the cow, and the-- and the-- and the horses, everything.
Then my mother says to me, you know what?
Don't stay with us.
You go.
You go.
I say, where I go?
I don't know where to go.
It was one guy over there, Polish guy.
He says, I take her to Krakow in ghetto.
I got my brother-- the oldest brother was in ghetto.
This time, the ghetto was closed.
You can't go-- not go-- not in, not out.
No.
All the Jewish people in one place-- it was a big place, you know, the-- not so big, but all the Jewish people was in the one place.
And I says-- and the guy says, yeah.
If you give me 3,000 zloty-- not dollars-- 3,000 zloty, I take her to Krakow.
No.
When you got to Krakow with me, I don't know you-- nothing.
You-- you go first, or I go first, and you go behind.
It was 40 kilometers to Krakow from this village.
So my father says, I have $20 I can give you-- no more.
I don't have any more.
So he says, no.
I wanted 3,000 zlotys.
The $20 he doesn't want.
So my father gives him the 3,000 zloty, and he went with me.
But I was afraid, you know, that the German they go in and back-- they recognize me or not.
So I take something, a piece of bread, another [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know how-- on the back-- and-- and milk.
And I put a-- a scarf like this.
Nobody recognized me.
But I couldn't walk so many kilometers.
You know, I says, I got like this.
I says, you know what?
Maybe we take the-- it was a tram by in Poland.
He said-- I says, I can't walk anymore.
He says, what?
You crazy?
If somebody recognize you-- and this was true.
And I said, you know what?
[NON-ENGLISH] was at the [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH] was like here, sometimes here.
Sometime you need a [NON-ENGLISH] another wagon.
A [NON-ENGLISH] is a horse.
Yeah.
Yeah, a horse.
And a carriage.
And a-- yeah.
But-- but this was covered.
And he did.
So where can I go?
You can't go in ghetto.
And then I says, you know what?
In this same strip, where the ghetto was, my brother used to work-- live on this street.
I says, take me over there to the superintendent.
And he took me.
See, he got two kids.
She's saw me.
Oh!
You know how the Pollacks-- I'm afraid, you know.
But the kids are not here.
I take you and put you in the closet.
And-- but if the kids enter here, they cannot recognize you.
They can go and tell them.
I was two days in-- in the closet.
And then I says, go and tell my brother that I am here.
He was sure nobody's alive, because he-- he knows everybody.
And they hear the-- the bum!
Bombs!
And here.
I am here.
He says, listen.
Keep her the night, but I gonna try and tell one from the other man.
This was the Jewish--
The oldest, Sigmund.
Yeah, the oldest.
So he says, I take her.
I can't take her now.
It has to be legal, you now?
And before, when the ghetto is in the door, was a other German.
He was so good.
He was killed in-- before the war-- about two days before.
Yeah.
But he got a-- a Jewish woman, and he keep her in someplace with the-- with her kids-- a 3-- 3-years-old boy.
I don't.
He was-- he didn't see nothing.
When you go to work outside, they-- the people, they bring bread, you know.
But he didn't see.
He covered the Jewish woman.
And her husband was with me together in Brunnlitz-- the woman's husband.
Before the war, somebody saw him, tell the German here and here is a Jewish woman.
He put her here.
They killed-- killed him.
They killed her, and the baby.
For two days.
Then--
Oh yes.
She given me, yeah.
Because-- and then the kids, they went to the school.
They were not at home.
So she give me, yeah-- about two nights.
I was so quiet.
I couldn't say nothing, you know.
And she was happy when the kids, they go out.
When I came to around other man.
This is the Jewish police, you know.
He take me in-- and let me in-- the German.
He let me in-- not even to my brother.
My sister-in-law, what I got, I says, better than when I was in my town.
And I was killed.
Must help.
She couldn't see me.
She doesn't want to see me here.
Then you can go like this.
You have to work.
You have to work.
If I have to go to work, I have to have a machine to-- a sewing machine.
And I didn't got-- on where?
Tell me, your sister-in-law said you had to go to work?
You can't-- no.
You-- when you was in ghetto, you have to go to work.
Everybody has to work!
If not, they killed you!
So my brother got somebody in Madritsch where I-- they take me over there.
Over there it was the Jewish people are sewing.
This was the uniform what I told--
No, no, no.
Yeah!
You know the-- the-- the people that he knows.
So he takes me over there, and I was over there.
They didn't know I can do some things or not.
So they try.
They give me first, I remember like now, the collars for men's shirts.
The collars was for Madritsch-- on-- the chef from the factory.
And I make the collars.
One looks at the one that says, oh, beautiful, so nice.
They couldn't believe that they-- I sit in the first [NON-ENGLISH].
And I got a machine.
I got everything.
And the supervisors-- this was Jewish people.
And then they give me, you know, pyjamas to do for Madritsch.
I did.
I was the first over there-- the first.
They need something, they came to me, you know.
And for a while, we was-- we lived in-- in ghetto, and we go to outside.
This was not too far from ghetto.
From ghetto to Madritsch was in Krakow.
And we went over there.
But now a few months--
This was the year-- 1939 or '40-- '40.
And we went over there.
And then from Madritsch, then he got six shirts to make for [NON-ENGLISH].
If these-- if some stitch is not right, he can kill you.
He looks like this on-- you know?
The [NON-ENGLISH]-- the [NON-ENGLISH] was the ship-- the chef from the whole concentration camp Krakow-Plaszow.
And we went-- before we went to work in Krakow-Plaszow.
We didn't go when they liquidated this ghetto, you know.
So we went to Krakow-Plaszow.
Exactly, I don't remember the date.
When we came--
There was--
--no!
They came.
And then everybody has to go in concentration camp-- no more ghetto.
In ghetto when we was-- when it was-- there's a liquidate the ghetto, got to over there.
It was unbelievable what they doing with the Jewish people.
They kill.
What I saw-- I was scared to look.
I saw this.
They take a baby, a small baby from third floor.
This is not the third floor of like here.
This is the second, third.
This is like here the fourth floor.
They ripped in the hand and they throw down.
This I saw in ghetto when they liquidated.
Yeah.
They killed them.
They killed them.
They go house by house.
They look.
Or somebody is not inside, who was inside, they kill.
German, German.
Oh.
